January 14,
1994
Robert E. Leiri i gh
Olson, Bagel,
Fong, Leidigh,
Waters,
and Fishburn
Capitol Bank Center
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File Nos.
I-93—348a and
I—93—406a
Dear Mr. Leidigh:
This is in response to your letter seeking follow—up advice
pertaining to your requests for advice, Leidigh Advice Letter No.
1—93—348 and Leidigh Advice Letter,
No.
1—93-406.
You made the
requests on behalf of the Senate Rules Committee pertaining to
Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
Since
your request for follow—up advice also does not name a specific
public official on whose behalf you make your request
1
we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.
4
You ask for clarification as to whether it is permissible
under Section 89001 of the Act for separate items,
which
independently meet an exception under Regulation 18901 (b),
to be
included in the same mailing.
In the Leidigh Advice Letter No.
1—93—406,
our conclusion
stated that Section 89001 does not prohibit a mailing where an
exception to the “mass mailing” prohibitions applies independently
to each item under Regulation 18901(b).
However, the summary of
the facts in the letter indicate the mailing in question would
1
Government Code Sections
81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


‘JUL
rj_je
j—j.—.’io
I—93—406a
Page 2
contain no other mention of the Senator’ s name inside the envelope
other than the two—sided insert discussed in the letter.
Thus,
you seek clarification regarding the independent application o an
exception under Regulation 18901(b)
to separate items.
Our conclusion in the Leidigh Advice Letter,
No.
1—93-406,
correctly suinmarizs our advice with respect to the mailing of
separate items,
and is consistent with our advice in the Leidigh
Advice Letter,
No.f
1—93—348.
There we concluded that Section
89001 does not prohibit a mailing of three separate items in the
same envelope where an exception to the
“mass mailing”
prohibitions applies to each item included in the mailing.
Thus, as correctly summarized in your letter,
a letterhead
memorandum sent by a State Senator which meets one of the
exceptions of Regulation 18901(b)
may be included as a separate
item in the same envelope as an item which is independently
excluded from the prohibitions of Section 89001.
I apologize for any confusion caused by restatement of the
facts in the Leidigh Advice Letter,
No.
1—93-406.
I trust this
letter addresses your concerns.
If you have any further questions
regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
/
-/
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

sections.question: Whether it is permissible under Section 89001 of the Act for separate items, which independently meet an exception under Regulation 18901(b), to be included in the same mailing.
sections.conclusion: Section 89001 does not prohibit a mailing where an exception to the 'mass mailing' prohibitions applies independently to each item under Regulation 18901(b). A letterhead memorandum sent by a State Senator which meets one of the exceptions of Regulation 18901(b) may be included as a separate item in the same envelope as an item which is independently excluded from the prohibitions of Section 89001.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks clarification on whether multiple separate items that each independently qualify for exceptions under Regulation 18901(b) may be included together in a single mailing without violating Section 89001's mass mailing prohibitions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that multiple separate items may be included in the same mailing if each item independently qualifies for an exception under Regulation 18901(b), and that this conclusion is consistent across the referenced advice letters (I-93-348 and I-93-406).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a follow-up informal advice letter clarifying and reaffirming conclusions from two prior advice letters (I-93-348 and I-93-406). The letter addresses confusion arising from factual restatement in the prior correspondence. Informal assistance noted as not providing immunity under Government Code Section 83114.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether it is permissible under Section 89001 of the Act for separate items, which independently meet an exception under Regulation 18901(b), to be included in the same mailing.

CONCLUSION: Section 89001 does not prohibit a mailing where an exception to the 'mass mailing' prohibitions applies independently to each item under Regulation 18901(b). A letterhead memorandum sent by a State Senator which meets one of the exceptions of Regulation 18901(b) may be included as a separate item in the same envelope as an item which is independently excluded from the prohibitions of Section 89001.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides follow-up clarification on informal advice regarding Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act, confirming that separate mailing items may be combined in a single envelope if each item independently qualifies for an exception under Regulation 18901(b).
